title: ANALYTICAL CHEMISTRY OF THORIUM. PART IX. DELAYED PRECIPITATION OR PRECIPITATION FROM HOMOGENEOUS SOLUTION
author: C. LAKSHMAN RAO
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description: Two new reagents for the estimation of thorium and its separation from cerite earths have been described. One, ammonium picrate, precipitates thorium completely in the  \(p\) H range 4.8 to 5.2 on heating for about 15 minutes on a boiling water-bath. This precipitation takes place .  gradually during heating and exhibits a behaviour similar to that of precipitation from homogeneous solutioa studied by Willard and co-workers. The second reagent, ammonium salt of 2 :4-dinitrophenol functions satis­factorily in the  \(p\) H range 4.8 to 5.2. 'Thorium in monazite has been successfully estimated employing both reagents.
steps_raw: Two new reagents for the estimation of thorium and its separation from cerite earths have been described. One, ammonium picrate, precipitates thorium completely in the  \(p\) H range 4.8 to 5.2 on heating for about 15 minutes on a boiling water-bath. This precipitation takes place .  gradually during heating and exhibits a behaviour similar to that of precipitation from homogeneous solutioa studied by Willard and co-workers. The second reagent, ammonium salt of 2 :4-dinitrophenol functions satis­factorily in the  \(p\) H range 4.8 to 5.2. 'Thorium in monazite has been successfully estimated employing both reagents.
